coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots from spices for determination of sunset yellow in beverages
coredata.dc:creator: Kaur, Amandeep
Gupta, Usha
Hasan, Imran
Muhammad, Raeesh
Ahmad Khan, Rais
coredata.dc:description: Sunset yellow is synthetic azo dye used to color food products in food industry. Sunset yellow has no toxicity if taken in permissible amounts. The daily acceptable intake of dye is 2.5 mg/Kg as per WHO health guidelines. Above the permissible limit, sunset yellow dye is dangerous posing serious threat to human health worldwide leading to multiorgan (gastric, immunological, neurological) failure related symptoms especially in children can cause DNA damage. Other symptoms include ADHD like behavior in children. This has led to Food Safety Agencies to regulate permissible limit of sunset yellow in food products. Therefore, it is necessary to develop method for the removal of dye in different foodstuffs. Nano-sized fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have been synthesized from spices (fenugreek, cloves and cumin) under hydrothermal conditions. Characterization has been done through UV–Vis, TEM, FT-IR and XRD spectroscopy. Results reveals that CDs possess high water solubility due to surface rich hydroxyl groups, excellent photo-stability and are non-toxic. There was strong quenching of fluorescence intensity (F0/F) of CDs in the presence of sunset yellow food dye. Further, there was no effect to fluorescence intensity with the presence of interferences. The linear concentration range obtained was 0.25 µM-60.0 µM with the detection limit of 0.20 µM. The calculated relative standard deviation (RSD) for determination of sunset yellow was found to be 0.21%. The present method was applied successfully for the determination of sunset yellow dye in various food samples with recovery values of 99.8 and 103.6%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs
Hydrothermal process
Sunset yellow
Fluorescence
Quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection and removal of sunset yellow

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: RAK extend their appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project RSP-2021/400, at King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia. The authors appreciatively recognize Department of Chemistry, Chandigarh University Mohali and Punjabi University Patiala for providing laboratory and infrastructure facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis absorption and Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs. (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs in (i) presence of sunset yellow (ii) absence of sunset yellow.
SEM Images of CDs at (a) 50 μm (b) 10 μm (c) TEM image of CDs showing particle size with (d) gaussian distribution of the particle for statistical analysis.
Effect of varying pH on fluorescence intensity for detection of sunset yellow.
(a) Plot of intensity vs wavelength for 0–40 µM SY (b) Plot of F/F0 vs concentration (µM).
(a) Plot of effect of time (0–10 min) (b) Plot of F/F0 vs wavelength.
Plot of F/F0 vs effect of interfering substance.

(a) Emission spectra of CDs for sunset yellow analysis at varying concentrations in food samples (Purchased from local grocery store) (b) Fluorescence intensity curve in relationship to volume of dye.
Experimental results of current method in contrast to with those of literature for sunset yellow analysis.
Precision and recovery results of analysis in beverages.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots from spices for determination of sunset yellow in beverages

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Punjabi University Patiala, Punjab 147002, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Punjabi University Patiala, Punjab 147002, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environmental Research Lab, Department of Chemistry, Chandigarh University, Mohali, Punjab 140413, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Environmental Research Lab, Department of Chemistry, Chandigarh University, Mohali, Punjab 140413, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Energy Engineering, Gyeongsang National University, Jinju, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Energy Engineering, Gyeongsang National University, Jinju, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, King Saud University, Riyadh 11451, KSA, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, King Saud University, Riyadh 11451, KSA, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sunset yellow is synthetic azo dye used to color food products in food industry. Sunset yellow has no toxicity if taken in permissible amounts. The daily acceptable intake of dye is 2.5 mg/Kg as per WHO health guidelines. Above the permissible limit, sunset yellow dye is dangerous posing serious threat to human health worldwide leading to multiorgan (gastric, immunological, neurological) failure related symptoms especially in children can cause DNA damage. Other symptoms include ADHD like behavior in children. This has led to Food Safety Agencies to regulate permissible limit of sunset yellow in food products. Therefore, it is necessary to develop method for the removal of dye in different foodstuffs. Nano-sized fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have been synthesized from spices (fenugreek, cloves and cumin) under hydrothermal conditions. Characterization has been done through UV–Vis, TEM, FT-IR and XRD spectroscopy. Results reveals that CDs possess high water solubility due to surface rich hydroxyl groups, excellent photo-stability and are non-toxic. There was strong quenching of fluorescence intensity (F0/F) of CDs in the presence of sunset yellow food dye. Further, there was no effect to fluorescence intensity with the presence of interferences. The linear concentration range obtained was 0.25 µM-60.0 µM with the detection limit of 0.20 µM. The calculated relative standard deviation (RSD) for determination of sunset yellow was found to be 0.21%. The present method was applied successfully for the determination of sunset yellow dye in various food samples with recovery values of 99.8 and 103.6%.

ce:section.ce:para: Food dyes have been the basis of food industries. Food manufacturers add food colors during food processing to produce appealing-colored products or to add flavors to their food products for consumers, which may lead to serious illness. They can be both from natural origin or synthetic. Synthetic food dyes find wider applications than natural one because of their excellent photostability, low oxidation and cost-effective production methods. Sunset yellow is a synthetic azo dye which is disodium salt of 2-Hydroxy-1- (4-sulfonato phenyl azo naphthalene-6-sulfonate) and is yellow in color. It is commonly used in cocktails, sweet puddings medicines, bakery and variety of other products [1]. Daily acceptable intake of sunset yellow is below 2.5 mg/Kg according to WHO guidelines [2,3]. Beyond this limit, this dye is dangerous posing serious threat to human health worldwide leading to multiorgan (gastric, immunological, neurological) failure related symptoms especially in children [4,5]. It is necessary to control the total routine intake of sunset yellow food dye so that the daily acceptable intake (ADI) of the dye is not exceeded. Therefore, it is necessary to generate efficient techniques for determination of sunset yellow dye to ensure food safety. Many researchers have been focusing on use of crown ethers [6,7], composite materials [8–10], ligand-based conjugates [11,12], optical composite materials [13], mesoporous composite materials [14–16].

Various analytical procedures have been followed to analyze the contents of food dyes in different food samples such as UV–Vis’s spectroscopy [17], electrochemical method [18], diffuse reflectance FTIR spectroscopy [19], solid phase extraction [20] and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [21]. But all these reported techniques are not efficient due to requirement of tedious sample pretreatment and delicate instrumentation. Therefore, there is urgent need of development of method, which requires easy sample preparation, simple and reliable instrumentation.

Fluorescent nanomaterial such as carbon nano dots (CDs) due to their unique characteristics. are increasingly employed in various fields of research to control environmental pollution [22,23]. These CDs with size ≤ 10 nm, consist of sp2 hybridized carbon core and surface hydroxyl and carboxyl group [17] found applications in wider fields such as owing to easy preparation, non-toxicity, higher water solubility, photostability, and biocompatibility [18]. Broad fields of applications include food industry, cell imaging [24–26]. Other applications of CDs are in the field of nanodevices, cell imaging, and in biomedicine [27–29]. Abundant carbon sources provide excellent raw materials for synthesis of CDs such as small organic molecules, egg shell, garlic, coriander leaves, onion, fruit juice, milk, rose-heart radish [30] have been reported in literature as natural sources of carbon for synthesis of CDs. Originally carbon is black solid having low water solubility and weakly fluorescent. Therefore, surface is passivated by different functional groups using various organic moieties to make CDs fluorescent in the visible and IR region of the spectrum.

Till date, various routes have been explored for the synthesis of CDs, which are reported in literature such as hydrothermal carbonization, [31] arc discharge, [32] plasma treatment, [33] laser ablation, [34] solvothermal treatment, [35] electrochemical oxidation, and microwave pyrolysis [36,37]. Hydrothermal method is more efficient over other reported physical methods in terms of cost, eco-friendly and non-requirement of any specific equipment [19,20]. Currently, hydrothermal carbonization of different natural sources has been successfully applied to prepare CDs. Also, the chemical components present in the natural sources plays a major role in assigning surface functional groups on the CDs [38–41]. Therefore, the emission wavelength of the CDs prepared from different natural sources has been found to be different. In present work, a simple and green method of hydrothermal carbonization for the synthesis of CDs from spices (fenugreek, cumin and cloves) as carbon precursor has been used. Therefore, fluorescent CDs offer as an effective nanomaterial for detection of sunset yellow in beverages.

All the spices (fenugreek, clove and cumin) for experimental work were purchased from goal market (Punjabi University, Patiala, India). All the used chemical reagents were of analytical grade, purchased from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai, India. Millipore water was used during the experimental work and was provided by Millipore System (18.2MΩ cm) of Sophisticated Instrument Department of Punjabi University, Patiala, Punjab, India.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the synthesized carbon dots was performed at 20kv as an accelerating voltage using Hitachi H-8100 transmission electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan) for morphological studies. UV–Vis absorption measurements were recorded on Shimadzu UV–Vis 2500 spectrophotometer. The fluorescence studies were done on Shimadzu RF-5301 PC Spectro fluorophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) using 1 cm path length. Fourier Transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed on F-7000 Shimadzu spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). All the pH measurements were done using Cp-901 digital century pH meter with combined glass electrode.

The hydrothermal method for the synthesis of carbon dots is green as compared to other strategies followed for synthesis of nanomaterials [42–45]. The Carbon dots were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of spices [20]. In a typical procedure, 2 g of each fenugreek, cloves and cumin were weighed and added to a mortar. Then this mixture is powdered with the help of pestle to fine powder. 6 g of these spices were added into 30 ml of double distilled water taken in a 100 ml beaker. After this, the solution mixture was placed in Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated constantly at 120 °C for 4hrs. The autoclave was cooled to room temperature as heating is over and the brown colored solution containing carbon dots were collected. The upper light brown solution was filtered with the help of 0.22 μm membrane filter. Finally, the contents of obtained aqueous solution consisting of C-dots was added to 100 ml volumetric flask and stored at low temperature in dark for experimental work. Further quantum measurements were done and the results reveal 3.98% yield.

In a typical procedure, to a 400 μL of C-dot solution, Britton-Robinson solution of pH 6.0 was added. Then appropriate volume of sunset yellow dye. Finally, the solution was added to 10 ml-calibrated flask and diluted up to mark with Millipore water. A fluorescence spectrum of the resulting solution was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 441 nm and fluorescence intensity (F/F0) was calculated from the resulting spectra.

Absorption and emission spectra of CDs are shown in Fig. 1 (a). The maximum absorption peak was observed at 298 nm. This is due to n → π* transition of carbonyl group and π → π* transition of alkene group [46]. Emission spectra of synthesized CDs show wavelength dependent emission. It is clear from the Fig. 1 (a), as the excitation wavelength increases from 441 to 520 nm, emission wavelength shifts from 510 to 618 nm. Fluorescence emission occurs is at 503 nm under excitation of 441 nm. It clearly shows that CDs are highly fluorescent and dependence of excitation upon emission intensity. Absorption, emission and maximum excitation spectra of synthesized CDs is shown in Fig. 1 (a), and the results reveals that emission peak is smaller than that of excitation indicating energy loss when excited CDs comes to lower vibrational energy level [47].

Further the fluorescence studies of CD were carried out in presence of sunset yellow Fig. 1 b (i) and absence of sunset yellow Fig. 1 b (ii). It can be seen that in presence of sunset yellow CD indicates higher intensity at 441 nm wavelength under fluorescence.

The morphological characterization of the synthesized CDs was done using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as shown in Fig. 2 . CDS are well aggregated with spherical distribution and are homogenous as shown in Fig. 3 (a, b). Further to understand shape and size of synthesized CDs, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) was done. Fig. 2(c) clearly shows a narrow distribution of the spherical CDs. To understand the size distribution of the CDs statistical studies were done based upon TEM images at different sites. Fig. 2(d) reveals the size distribution histogram showing major part of the CDs lie between 5 and 10 nm.

To understand the nature of surface functional groups of synthesized CDs, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was done as shown in Fig. 3. And the results reveal characteristic absorption peak aroused at 3436 cm−1 is due to –OH stretching vibration mode, The absorption peak at 3069 cm−1 was attributed to stretching vibration of O–H bond, while the absorption peak at 2928 cm−1 was related to C–H and O–H vibration mode. The 1560 cm−1 peak was attributed to the vibration of CC bond [48]. The absorption peak at 1483 cm−1 was assigned to the vibration of CC, and C–OH group. The small absorption peak at 1221 cm−1 corresponds to the vibration of C–O–C, C–H and C–OH bonds [49]. And the peak at 1399 cm−1 is related to COO – group. These indicate that CDs have bulk of different functional groups which lies at the fringe of CDs and improves the fluorescence yield.

Fluorescence spectra: - Further emission was done (Fig. 6) under different excitation wavelengths from 440 nm to 520 nm. Results showed that with increase in excitation wavelength, fluorescence intensity decreases. Therefore, it is clear from the Fig. 6, absorption occurs at higher intensity as concentration of amaranth increases, which indicates formation of amaranth-C-dots complex. Thus, C-dots fluorescence intensity depends on excitation wavelength. These results are in accordance with Vaibhav Kumar [50,51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection and removal of sunset yellow
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure, C-dots solution was added to a solution containing sunset yellow dye and the pH was adjusted by varying amounts of HCl or NaOH (0.05 M). Various experimental conditions were optimized to study the accuracy, reliability, analytical performance of the applied method is, reaction pH, concentration of sunset yellow dye, effect of time etc were important to analyse sunset yellow [52]. This method of analysis eliminates the need of time-consuming reactions requiring materials for dye detection [53]. This method of analysis eliminates the need of costly catalysts for dye detection.

The fluorescence intensity of C-dots was evaluated in presence of varying concentrations of C-dots from 0 to 40 µM. (Fig. 6 ). Increasing the concentration of sunset yellow, fluorescence intensity of C-dots was decreased at 441 nm. This is because of dynamic quenching mechanism that can be explained that the excited state of C-dots when return to the ground state by the collision between the dye and C-dots by energy transfer or the mechanism of charge transfer [19], and the process can be represented by a simple equation:
A* + Q → A + Q or A* + Q → A + Q
where A is CDs, Q is a quencher and * designates an excited state.

The kinetics of this process follows the Stern–Volmer relationship [55–57]:
F0 /F = 1 + kqτ0 [Q]

Because of the dynamic quenching mechanism in which surface OH, –COOH, –NH groups are quenched by sunset yellow quencher. With increase in dye concentration, more surface groups are occupied by dye and therefore fluorescence intensity decreases. Thus, increasing dye concentration act as fluorescence quencher to fluorescent C-dots and therefore, it is clarified that the system is sensitive to sunset yellow dye detection using C-dots which is advantageous as food dyes exist in trace levels in food stuffs [58]. Fig. 6 (b) shows dependence of fluorescence quenching on sunset yellow dye concentration.

The time dependent (0–10 min) fluorescence spectrum of C-dots for detection of sunset yellow is shown in Fig. 7 . It is clear from the Fig. 7(b), that after 9 min, straight line is obtained in the curve. This is because afterwards that, the free functional sites on the surface C-dots are lowered because of weak interactions between dye and functional group of C-dots and the steady fluorescence intensity becomes constant due to equilibrium [59]. Therefore, 10 min are required for the reaction between C-dots and sunset yellow.

To study the effect of selectivity of C-dots towards sunset yellow at concentration of 4.5 µM in the presence of different interfering foreign ions and food dyes such as brilliant blue, ponceau 4r, rhodamine b, allura red, Ca2+, Fe2+, K+, Na+, Mg2+ was studied (Fig. 8 ). Different foreign substances were added to sample solution at higher amounts than sunset yellow. Results showed much lower intensity is observed for sunset yellow dye when different interfering dyes are present. Recoveries were found to be ≥ 99.9%. Therefore, all these results reveal accuracy of the proposed method and this shows outstanding selectivity of C-dots towards sunset yellow dye.

Further, comparison of experimental results of present method to that of reported in literature is shown in Table 1 . It is clear from the table that proposed method of fluorescence analysis of sunset yellow in beverages offers a wide linear range over all other methods of sunset yellow determination. The relative standard deviation values are lower than the other methods reported in literature. Also, all other methods require sophisticated instrumentation and complex operations for routine analysis. Therefore, present method of sunset yellow analysis involves synthesis of fluorescent CDs, which require simple instrumentation, low-cost materials and quick response and is reliable. Thus, it is far better for fast sunset yellow analysis.Table 2.

Food samples (beverages) were purchased from the local grocery store. Pretreated samples were filtered through 0.22 µm membrane and then centrifuged for 20 min at 12,000 rpm. The resultant solution was spiked with varying concentration of sunset yellow from 10 to 30μg/mL. CDs show exhibit good ability towards determination of sunset yellow in food samples is shown in Fig. 9 (b). It was observed that Fluorescence intensity decreases with increase in concentration of dye. Calibration curves for determination of dye in food samples were obtained by plotting F/F0 versus concentration of dye. Besides interferences, system is able to detect concentration of dye. Therefore, it is helpful for the detection of even lower concentrations of sunset yellow in various food samples [60,61].

The suitable carbon source(spices) was used to synthesize C-dots following hydrothermal carbonization for efficient detection and removal of sunset yellow from beverages. The specific color was formed when sunset yellow dye is added to C-dots solution. This gives an extra advantage for dye detection without using highly sophisticated apparatus. Therefore, the linear concentration range of 0.25 µg/L-60 µg/L was calculated with detection limit of 0.20 µg/L. The calculated relative standard deviation (RSD) was found to be 0.21% and method is exclusively selective. In comparison to other traditional methods, this method was green, rapid, economic and sensitive towards the sunset yellow dye at optimum experimental protocol. The fluorescent properties of the C-dots worsened to at higher pH regions. Decreasing the solution pH led to an increase the sunset yellow removal and the mild acidic pH 6.0 was chosen both in the detection and removal operation. The data suggested that the provided results are encouraging for the real application in large-scale for the sunset yellow dye containing food samples. Therefore, the detection and removal of sunset yellow by the fluorescent C-dots is an alternative route, being economically beneficial towards environment because synthesis of C-dots by hydrothermal method is cost effective, green and has no toxicity. This factor eliminates the need to reuse CDs which requires costly organic chemicals. Therefore, carbon dots with extraordinary properties serve as good material for detection of sunset yellow dye and other hazardous components in commercial food samples. As the source for synthesis of CDs is natural, therefore, method is economical, green, and biocompatible.

Amandeep Kaur: Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Usha Gupta: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Supervision. Imran Hasan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Rais Ahmad Khan: Methodology, Resources, Data curation, Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Raeesh Muhammad: Methodology, Software, Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: RAK extend their appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project RSP-2021/400, at King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia. The authors appreciatively recognize Department of Chemistry, Chandigarh University Mohali and Punjabi University Patiala for providing laboratory and infrastructure facilities.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous Determination of Color Additives Tartrazine and Allura Red in Food Products by Digital Image Analysis
Talanta

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical Fabrication of a Novel ZnO/Cysteic Acid Nanocomposite Modified Electrode and Its Application to Simultaneous Determination of Sunset Yellow and Tartrazine

Talanta

Anal. Lett.
